did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a modified Visual Geometry Group 16-layer (VGG-16) architecture for dermoscopic image analysis to extract spatial features, and a gradient boosting algorithm (LightGBM) for classifying biochemical profiles derived from Raman spectra. These AI models were used for the binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign pigmented neoplasms.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of Raman spectra and dermoscopic images from 338 skin lesion samples (65 malignant melanomas, 107 seborrheic keratoses, and 166 nevi). It describes the development and validation of new diagnostic models using specific algorithms (MCR-ALS, LightGBM, CNN) and reports new quantitative results, including ROC AUC values and confidence intervals, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multidimensional Analysis of Dermoscopic Images and Spectral Information for the Diagnosis of Skin Tumors
brief_summary: This study developed a multidimensional diagnostic model for distinguishing malignant melanoma from benign pigmented neoplasms by integrating Raman spectroscopy and dermoscopic image analysis. The dataset comprised 338 histologically confirmed skin lesions, including 65 malignant melanomas, 107 seborrheic keratoses, and 166 nevi. Spectral profiles were extracted using multivariate curve resolution alternating least squares and classified via a LightGBM algorithm, while spatial features were analyzed using a modified VGG-16 convolutional neural network. The individual models achieved ROC AUCs of 0.82 and 0.87, respectively, but their joint analysis via logistic regression yielded a superior binary classification model with an ROC AUC of 0.94 (95% CI: 0.90–0.98). The results demonstrate that combining spectral component features with spatial dermoscopic features significantly enhances the accuracy of skin cancer diagnosis compared to using either modality alone.

lesion_types_studied.details: 65 samples in the primary dataset; used as the positive class in binary classification against benign pigmented neoplasms.
107 samples in the primary dataset; grouped with nevi as 'benign pigmented neoplasms' for the binary classification task.
166 samples in the primary dataset; grouped with seborrheic keratoses as 'benign pigmented neoplasms' for the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign pigmented neoplasms (seborrheic keratoses and nevi).
all_task_types.details: The paper constructs a binary classification model to distinguish malignant melanoma (MM) from benign pigmented neoplasms (SK+NE). This was achieved using a gradient boosting algorithm (LightGBM) on Raman spectral features, a convolutional neural network (CNN) on dermoscopic images, and a joint logistic regression model combining both. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (MM vs. benign) with reported metrics including ROC AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Primary Experimental Dataset (Samara)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of histologically confirmed skin lesions collected using a portable Raman spectroscopy unit and a multispectral digital dermatoscope at Samara State Medical University and Samara Regional Clinical Oncology Dispensary. It includes Raman spectra and dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training the LightGBM model (spectral features) and the CNN model (dermoscopic images) via 10-fold cross-validation. For the CNN, a balanced subset was used (65 MM, 26 SK, 39 NE) with random sampling repeated 10 times.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 10-fold cross-validation process (10% excluded as test set, remaining used for training/validation cycles).
use.testing: Used for testing via 10-fold cross-validation (10% of objects excluded as test set in each fold).
use.other: Used for constructing the joint multidimensional model via logistic regression of predicted values from the RS and CNN models.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Skin Cancer MNIST)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Kaggle containing multi-source dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions (224x224 pixels, 24 bits, 96 dpi, .jpeg format).

use.training: Used for preliminary training (transfer learning) of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) before additional training on the primary experimental dataset.
use.other: Used to allow the CNN to learn general features (tumor boundary, size, shape) before fine-tuning on the specific experimental data.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the stability and performance of the LightGBM model, the CNN model, and the joint multidimensional logistic regression model, avoiding overfitting by repeatedly splitting the dataset into training and test sets.
To estimate the uncertainty of the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC AUC) metric for the RS model, the CNN model, and the multidimensional model.

To combine predicted values from the Raman spectral model and the dermoscopic image model for multidimensional analysis and binary classification of malignant melanoma versus benign pigmented neoplasms.
To calculate the final performance of the classification models by averaging the results over 10 experiments, accounting for the random nature of sampling in the cross-validation process.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported performance metrics (ROC AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) with 95% confidence intervals derived from 10-fold cross-validation to estimate uncertainty and stability, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare models or validate against a null hypothesis.

CNN Preliminary Training.notes: Used for transfer learning. The model was trained smoothly. Attention maps indicated the network paid attention to tumor boundary, size, and shape.

architecture_used.main_model: Modified Visual Geometry Group 16-layer (VGG-16) Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning: Preliminary training on the HAM10000 dataset (Skin Cancer MNIST), followed by additional training on the experimental dataset with the four upper layers frozen.

architecture_details.layers: Five alternating blocks of Conv2D and MaxPooling2D layers, followed by fully connected Dense layers.
architecture_details.activation_functions: ReLU for Conv2D and MaxPooling2D layers; Sigmoid for the output layer.
architecture_details.convolution_details: Conv2D layer uses a 3 × 3 sliding window to extract three-dimensional feature templates.
architecture_details.pooling_details: MaxPooling2D layers with a 2 × 2 window size to reduce feature map resolution by half.

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly stated (implied binary cross-entropy due to sigmoid output and classification task)
training_details.data_split: 8:1:1 ratio for training, validation, and test sets respectively (balanced classes).
training_details.epochs: Determined experimentally based on accuracy graphs for training and validation sets.

classification_or_output_head.task: Binary classification (Malignant Melanoma vs. Benign Pigmented Neoplasms)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with modified VGG-16 architecture
PLS-DA (Projection to Latent Structures Discriminant Analysis)
preprocessing_techniques: Raman Spectra Preprocessing: Cutting (cropping) the spectral range to 860–920 nm (corresponding to 1114–1874 cm⁻¹), smoothing using a Savitzky-Golay filter, and baseline removal to eliminate autofluorescence.

Dermoscopic Image Preprocessing: Images were recorded in polarized illumination mode; no specific computational preprocessing (e.g., resizing, normalization) is explicitly reported for the experimental dataset beyond the input dimensions (224 × 224 pixels) used for the CNN.

Data Augmentation/Resampling: For the CNN training on the experimental dataset, classes were balanced by randomly sampling from the larger class. For the HAM10000 dataset, geometric transformations (rotation by a random angle, shifts, zooming) were applied to increase the number of images in the smaller class (MM).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: For the CNN training on the primary experimental dataset, classes were balanced by randomly sampling from the larger class to match the smaller class (Malignant Melanoma). Specifically, 65 MM, 26 SK, and 39 NE samples were used. This process was repeated 10 times due to the random nature of sampling, and results were averaged.
Applied to the HAM10000 dataset to increase the number of images in the smaller class (Malignant Melanoma). Techniques included rotation by a random angle, shifts, and zooming.

conclusions: The joint analysis of Raman spectral component features and dermoscopic spatial features significantly enhances the diagnostic efficiency for distinguishing malignant melanoma from benign pigmented neoplasms compared to using either modality alone.

The multidimensional model, constructed via logistic regression of predicted values from the Raman spectral model (MCR+LightGBM) and the dermoscopic image model (CNN), achieved a superior binary classification performance with an ROC AUC of 0.94 (95% CI: 0.90–0.98).
The individual Raman spectral model (MCR+LightGBM) achieved an ROC AUC of 0.82 (95% CI: 0.76–0.89), which was significantly higher than the previously reported PLS-DA method (ROC AUC 0.69).

The individual dermoscopic image model (modified VGG-16 CNN) achieved an ROC AUC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.82–0.93), demonstrating that deep learning with transfer learning can effectively extract spatial features such as tumor boundary, size, shape, and relief.

The proposed approach allows for the extraction of physically interpretable diagnostic information (relative concentrations of skin components) from spectral data, addressing the 'black box' limitation of standard neural network applications in Raman spectroscopy.

The study acknowledges that the performance of the dermoscopic CNN model was somewhat unstable due to the small amount of data from the experimental multispectral digital dermatoscope, necessitating the use of transfer learning from the larger HAM10000 dataset.

The authors conclude that the developed multidimensional algorithm is a candidate for widespread use in decision control systems for skin tumor diagnosis, provided that the limitations regarding dataset size are addressed.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small primary dataset of 338 histologically confirmed lesions (65 malignant melanomas, 107 seborrheic keratoses, 166 nevi) collected from a single center in Samara, Russia, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population bias.

The dermoscopic CNN model exhibited instability due to the small size of the experimental dataset, necessitating transfer learning from the large public HAM10000 dataset; this introduces potential domain shift issues between the pre-training and target data distributions.

Class balancing was achieved via random undersampling of the larger classes (seborrheic keratoses and nevi) to match the smaller class (malignant melanoma) for CNN training, which discards potentially useful data and may affect model robustness.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to compare the performance of the multidimensional model against individual models or human experts, relying instead on overlapping confidence intervals and descriptive comparisons.

The multidimensional model's high specificity (1.00) at a threshold of 0.67 comes at the cost of reduced sensitivity (0.74), meaning it may miss some malignant melanomas if this threshold is used clinically, although the authors argue this is acceptable for screening followed by biopsy.
No external validation was performed on an independent dataset from a different institution or geographic region; validation was limited to 10-fold cross-validation within the single local dataset.

The Raman spectral preprocessing involved cropping the spectral range to 860–920 nm, which may exclude relevant biochemical information present in other spectral regions, potentially limiting the diagnostic potential of the spectral component.

The study acknowledges that the performance of the dermoscopic CNN was somewhat unstable due to the small amount of data from the experimental multispectral digital dermatoscope, highlighting a significant limitation in data availability for deep learning applications in this specific context.
